articles.title: Love, quest, adventure: the storytelling behind Xi Jinping’s speeches and China’s grand strategy
articles.description: My new research offers a new way to understand China’s grand strategy through plot devices and overarching narratives.

articles.title: MIT scientists create rare boron-oxygen ring once thought too unstable to exist
articles.description: Scientists at MIT isolated an unstable peroxide-like boron compound that can transfer oxygen atoms and react with carbon dioxide.

articles.title: A 59,000-year-old tooth reshapes what we know about Neanderthal dentistry
articles.description: Neanderthals used rock drills to treat an infected tooth, according to a study that pushes back the earliest known evidence of dentistry by more than 40,000 years.

Black Bear Fatally Mauls Uranium Contractor in Northern Canada
articles.description: The attack, at a remote uranium mining site in northern Saskatchewan, was only the fourth fatal black bear encounter in the province’s recorded history, officials said.

articles.title: Rutgers canceled his graduation speech — so he posted the whole thing on YouTube
articles.description: Biotech executive Rami Elghandour posted his full convocation remarks online after the university pulled his invitation over his pro-Palestinian social media posts.

articles.title: Record Contrarian Sentiment Signals Major Equity Breakout Ahead
articles.description: Market sentiment has reached levels not seen since major market bottoms, creating what technical analysts are calling a textbook contrarian setup. When investor pessimism reaches extreme levels, history suggests equity markets often stage their most powerful rallies, and current indicators point to such conditions forming across multiple asset classes. The foundation of any contrarian setup... The post Record Contrarian Sentiment Signals Major Equity Breakout Ahead appeared first on Equity Insider .

articles.title: Five takeaways from UC Berkeley’s new survey on psychedelics in society
articles.description: In this Q&A, postdoctoral research scientist Tyrone Sgambati unpacks some of the key findings from a sweeping new study on public perceptions of psychedelics. The post Five takeaways from UC Berkeley’s new survey on psychedelics in society appeared first on Berkeley News .

articles.title: Newly discovered myxobacterium in a cyanobacterial community performs photosynthesis
articles.description: Researchers at the Leibniz Institute DSMZ-German Collection of Microorganisms and Cell Cultures have conducted high-resolution investigations of the so-called cyanosphere, the "housing community" of cyanobacteria. The focus was on identifying previously unknown associated bacteria that play a crucial role in the vitality of the cyanobacteria. Particular attention was paid to a newly discovered myxobacterium capable of performing photosynthesis. The researchers led by Prof. Dr. Jörn Petersen from the Department of Microorganisms at the Leibniz Institute DSMZ published their findings in Environmental Microbiology Reports and ISME Communications.

articles.title: Analytical framework tracks response diversity over time, linking it to ecosystem stability
articles.description: An international research team led by National Taiwan University has developed a new analytical framework based on Empirical Dynamic Modeling. This framework can quantify how response diversity varies over time using time-series data. Published in Nature Communications, the study offers the first direct empirical evidence that greater response diversity helps stabilize total community biomass. This breakthrough addresses a longstanding methodological challenge in ecology: measuring the response diversity of natural communities solely from observational data.

articles.title: One of the Largest Physics Surveys Ever Finds No One Agrees on Anything
articles.description: 1,600 respondents answered 10 questions about central issues in physics, from the Big Bang to black holes, cosmic inflation, and quantum gravity. The results are...interesting.

articles.title: UC San Diego Undergraduate Research Students Use Data Science to Address Housing and Food Insecurity
articles.description: At the Halıcıoğlu Data Science Institute (HDSI), part of the School of Computing, Information and Data Sciences (SCIDS) at UC San Diego, students in the Undergraduate Research Scholarship Program aim to address critical local challenges through data-driven research.

articles.title: New Mathematical Framework Better Illustrates Complex Data Patterns
articles.description: Mathematicians with UC San Diego’s Halıcıoğlu Data Science Institute (HDSI) have unveiled a novel approach that could transform how scientists analyze and understand complex data sets across multiple fields.

articles.title: Chemists discover and isolate a new boron–oxygen molecule
articles.description: Oxygen is a cornerstone of chemistry, largely because it is so good at building the organic molecules that make up our world. Some oxygen-based compounds called peroxides are famous for being highly reactive—they act like oxygen delivery trucks, transferring atoms to other molecules. This process is essential for everything from creating new medicines to industrial manufacturing.

articles.title: An astrophysicist's take on the government's UAP files: 'Just more fuzzy blob videos'
articles.description: NPR's Scott Detrow talks with astrophysicist Adam Frank about the government's release of files related to Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena.

articles.title: Groundbreaking Study Uncovers How Our Brain Learns
articles.description: How do we learn new things? Neurobiologists using cutting-edge visualization techniques have revealed how changes across our synapses and neurons unfold. The findings depict how information is processed in our brain’s circuitry, offering insights for neurological disorders and brain-like AI systems.

articles.title: Neanderthals weren't so stupid after all! Study reveals how caveman 'dentists' used sophisticated stone drills to treat cavities 60,000 years ago
articles.description: They're commonly depicted as primitive and uncivilised, but Neanderthal 'dentists' used stone drills to treat cavities 60,000 years ago, a study reveals.

articles.title: Neanderthal Dentists Treated Cavities With Stone Drills. Yes, Really
articles.description: A 59,000-year-old Neanderthal molar contains evidence of a cavity removal procedure, offering fresh insight into these early humans' intelligence.

articles.title: More selective breeding might help flat-faced dogs to breathe easier
articles.description: Breeding programs could alter heritable dog characteristics to reduce the rates of brachycephalic obstructive airway syndrome (BOAS), a breathing disorder common in dogs such as Bulldogs, French Bulldogs and Pugs—according to a new study led by Dr. Joanna Jadwiga Ilska of the Royal Kennel Club, published May 13, 2026 in the open-access journal PLOS One.

articles.title: The first dentist: How a single tooth changes what we thought about Neanderthals
articles.description: Evidence of caries treatment on a 60,000-year-old tooth found in Russia pushes back the birth of dentistry, a skill that archaeologists didn't expect to encounter in Neanderthals

articles.title: First evidence of Neandertal dentistry found in ancient molar
articles.description: A 59,000-year-old Neandertal molar unearthed in Siberia was drilled with a stone tool – the earliest evidence of primitive dentistry.

articles.title: 'Exceptional' drilled tooth reveals Neanderthals practiced dentistry in Siberia 60,000 years ago
articles.description: A hole found in a 60,000-year-old Neanderthal tooth was likely made by a stone drill, making the discovery the oldest evidence of intentional dentistry to date.

Psyche Spacecraft Spies Mars Ahead of May 15th Gravitational Assist
articles.description: A close flyby past the Red Planet this week will send NASA’s Psyche mission on its way towards its final destination. The mission’s closest approach to Mars occurs on Friday, May 15th, when the spacecraft passes only 4,500 kilometers (2,800 miles) from the surface of the Red Planet. That’s just 1.3 Mars radii distant, inside the orbits of Phobos and Deimos.

articles.description: The Brookings Institution is a nonprofit public policy organization based in Washington, DC. Our mission is to conduct in-depth research that leads to new ideas for solving problems facing society at the local, national and global level.

articles.description: J. Nathan Matias, assistant professor of communication, is a co-author of “Auditing AI,” which offers AI users from all walks of life an introduction into AI evaluation, which is key for developing trust in the technology.

articles.description: Lithuanian officials hope restored peat bogs can reinforce the border in addition to locking away planet-warming carbon.

articles.title: Amazon's carbon clock is speeding up, and violent storms may be only part of why
articles.description: Tropical forests store more than 60% of the world's vegetation biomass and are among the most important ecosystems for regulating the global carbon cycle and climate. However, their regulatory role is greatly influenced by the forests' carbon residence time—how long carbon remains in the vegetation biomass pool before it is released again into the atmosphere. This figure is tied to the rate of biomass turnover—how quickly vegetation is replaced through growth and mortality.

articles.title: What Your Kitchen Sink Has in Common With Venus
articles.description: Turn on your kitchen tap and watch the water hit the sink. That split second where fast, shallow water suddenly slows and spreads is known as a hydraulic jump. Now imagine the same thing happening in the atmosphere of Venus, but stretched across 6,000 kilometres of sulphuric acid cloud. Researchers at the University of Tokyo have just revealed that this extraordinary phenomenon, the largest hydraulic jump ever identified in the Solar System, is responsible for a mysterious wave that has been sweeping around our neighbouring planet for years.

articles.title: The foods that stimulate the brain and release dopamine
articles.description: Scientists once thought flavanols helped the brain via absorption, but a new study suggests their astringent taste may directly activate the brain like exercise.

articles.title: Four People in a Pixel
articles.description: When NASA's Artemis II spacecraft carried four astronauts around the Moon earlier this year, the world's largest fully steerable radio telescope was quietly watching from a quiet valley in West Virginia. The Green Bank Telescope tracked the Orion capsule across 213,000 miles of empty space with a precision that would embarrass most speedometers and what it produced isn't just an engineering triumph. It's a glimpse of how the world's most sensitive ears are becoming indispensable to the future of human spaceflight.

articles.title: Elon Musk Calls Fully Reusable Rockets A 'Crazy Hard Problem' As SpaceX Pushes Starship V4
articles.description: SpaceX CEO Elon Musk has outlined that engine reliability is a crucial aspect for rocket reusability, being equally important to heat shield integrity. ‘Crazy Hard Problem’ Responding to user @GeniusGTX on X, who had shared a clip of Musk illustrating the importance of heat shields and figuring out a way to make reusable heat shields on rockets. data-variant="card" data-news-mode="manual" > Read Also: Ron DeSantis Praises Elon Musk's 'Pro-Human' AI Vision After SpaceX-Anthropic Partnership “High-cycle, reliable engine reflight without replacement, refurbishment or laborious inspection is about the same degree of difficulty as a rapidly reusable heat shield,” Musk said in his response. Musk then shared that there were “thousands” of other smaller challenges on the entire rocket structure that could affect the rocket’s functions. “This is a crazy hard problem,” he said. High-cycle, reliable engine reflight without replacement, refurbishment or laborious inspection is about the same degree of ... Full story available on Benzinga.com

articles.title: Ten years on, the Nagoya Protocol on sharing genetic resources is still confusing scientists—guidance now available
articles.description: More than a decade after the Nagoya Protocol, which aims to fairly share the benefits of utilizing genetic resources, became law, microbiologists and other scientists still face practical challenges and confusion. A new guide published by a team of European microbiologists provides universally applicable frameworks for anyone working with biological resources.
